articles.headline: Supreme Court Directs Delhi Government on Comprehensive Stray Dog Management Plan
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, on an appeal, modified its earlier order, directing the Delhi government to implement a comprehensive plan for managing stray dogs, allowing for the release of sterilized and immunized dogs.
• The initial order had called for all stray dogs to be transferred to animal shelters within eight weeks, citing public safety concerns due to dog bites and rabies.
• The modified directive emphasizes a balanced approach, prioritizing public health and safety while also ensuring humane treatment and welfare of animals.
• Key components of the revised plan include mandatory sterilization, immunization, identification, and a robust adoption program, alongside community engagement initiatives.
• This ruling comes amidst increasing incidents of dog bites and concerns from both residents and animal welfare organizations regarding the handling of stray populations.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population
Role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders
Judiciary
prelimsPoints: Supreme Court's role in public health and welfare issues.
Provisions related to animal welfare in India (e.g., Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960).
Animal Birth Control (Dogs) Rules, 2001 and subsequent updates.
Jurisdiction of state governments vs. central guidelines on animal management.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges of managing stray animal populations in urban areas, balancing public safety with animal rights.
Analyze the role of the judiciary in directing policy implementation and ensuring accountability of state governments in welfare issues.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current animal birth control programs and suggest measures for improvement.
Examine the ethical considerations and legal framework surrounding animal welfare in India.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic illustrating a dog being vaccinated, with the Supreme Court building in the background.
articles.trivia: The Animal Welfare Board of India (AWBI) was established in 1962 under Section 4 of The Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960, making it the first of its kind in the world.

articles.headline: Nation Commemorates 83rd Anniversary of Quit India Movement
articles.explainer:
• August 9, 2025, marks the 83rd anniversary of the Quit India Movement, a pivotal moment in India's struggle for independence against British colonial rule.
• Launched by Mahatma Gandhi in 1942, the movement called for an immediate end to British rule, with the slogan 'Do or Die'.
• The movement saw widespread civil disobedience, protests, and strikes across the country, despite the immediate arrest of major leaders.
• It galvanized the Indian masses and demonstrated their unwavering resolve for complete independence, significantly influencing the eventual British withdrawal.
• Various commemorative events, seminars, and pledges were held across the nation to remember the sacrifices of freedom fighters.
gsSubTopics: Modern Indian history from about the middle of the eighteenth century until the present- significant events, personalities, issues
The Freedom Struggle - its various stages and important contributors /contributions from different parts of the country
prelimsPoints: Date of Quit India Movement launch (August 8, 1942, resolution passed; movement started Aug 9).
Key leaders associated with the movement (Gandhi, Nehru, Patel, Azad, Aruna Asaf Ali, Jayaprakash Narayan).
Slogan 'Do or Die'.
Reasons for its launch (failure of Cripps Mission, WWII impact, growing impatience).
Impact of the movement despite leadership arrests.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the Quit India Movement in accelerating India's journey to independence.
Discuss the nature and extent of mass participation in the Quit India Movement, highlighting its strengths and weaknesses.
Evaluate the role of Mahatma Gandhi's leadership and the 'Do or Die' call in shaping the trajectory of the movement.
Compare and contrast the Quit India Movement with earlier phases of the freedom struggle.
articles.imageDescription: A historical photograph of a large crowd demonstrating during the Quit India Movement.
articles.trivia: The 'Quit India' slogan itself was coined by Yusuf Meherally, a socialist and trade unionist, who also coined the slogan 'Simon Go Back'.

articles.headline: Union Cabinet Approves New National Logistics Policy to Boost Economic Competitiveness
articles.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet today approved the much-anticipated National Logistics Policy (NLP), aimed at reducing logistics costs and improving India's global trade competitiveness.
• The policy seeks to integrate various modes of transport, streamline regulatory frameworks, and leverage technology for efficient goods movement across the country.
• Key objectives include reducing logistics costs from the current 13-14% of GDP to single digits, enhancing multimodal connectivity, and creating a data-driven decision support system.
• The NLP is expected to boost manufacturing, create employment opportunities, and improve the 'Ease of Doing Business' by creating a unified logistics ecosystem.
• It will facilitate faster last-mile delivery, reduce transaction costs, and enable better planning for perishable goods, benefiting farmers and consumers alike.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Investment models
prelimsPoints: Objectives of the National Logistics Policy (NLP).
Target for reducing logistics costs as a percentage of GDP.
Key pillars/components of the NLP (e.g., ULIP, digital integration).
Related government initiatives like PM Gati Shakti National Master Plan.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the National Logistics Policy in achieving India's economic growth targets and enhancing global competitiveness.
Discuss the challenges faced by India's logistics sector and how the new policy aims to address them.
Examine the potential impact of the NLP on various sectors such as manufacturing, agriculture, and e-commerce.
Critically evaluate the role of digital transformation and infrastructure development in the successful implementation of the NLP.
articles.imageDescription: A visual representation of interconnected logistics networks, including roads, railways, and ports.
articles.trivia: India's logistics sector is one of the largest globally, valued at over $200 billion, and is expected to grow significantly with policy reforms.

articles.headline: India-Japan Strategic Dialogue Concludes, Emphasizes Indo-Pacific Cooperation and Economic Ties
articles.explainer:
• The annual India-Japan Strategic Dialogue concluded today, reaffirming both nations' commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific region.
• Discussions focused on enhancing defense cooperation, maritime security, economic partnerships, and collaboration in emerging technologies.
• Both sides stressed the importance of upholding international law, particularly UNCLOS, and ensuring freedom of navigation and overflight in critical sea lanes.
• New initiatives were announced to boost investment, promote clean energy projects, and strengthen supply chain resilience between the two countries.
• The dialogue underscored the growing strategic alignment between India and Japan as key pillars of regional stability and economic growth.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: Key areas of cooperation between India and Japan (defense, economy, technology, maritime).
Importance of Indo-Pacific concept for both nations.
UNCLOS (United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea).
Related groupings like Quad (Quadrilateral Security Dialogue).
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolution and significance of the India-Japan Special Strategic and Global Partnership in the context of changing geopolitical dynamics in the Indo-Pacific.
Discuss how India and Japan are collaborating to ensure a stable and prosperous Indo-Pacific region, addressing common security challenges.
Examine the economic dimensions of India-Japan relations, including trade, investment, and infrastructure development.
Evaluate the role of bilateral and multilateral frameworks (e.g., Quad) in shaping India's foreign policy towards East Asia.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and Japan displayed during a diplomatic meeting.
articles.trivia: Japan is a significant investor in India's infrastructure, notably contributing to the Mumbai-Ahmedabad High-Speed Rail project.

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Conducts Critical Test of Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) Prototype
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced the successful completion of a critical test for its Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) prototype, marking a significant step towards cost-effective space access.
• The test involved a complex landing experiment simulating a re-entry scenario, demonstrating autonomous navigation, guidance, and control systems.
• RLVs are crucial for reducing the cost of space missions by allowing the same launch vehicle components to be used multiple times, similar to aircraft.
• This achievement positions India among a select few nations developing advanced reusable space technologies, enhancing its capabilities in space exploration and commercial launches.
• Future plans include further tests on orbital re-entry and hypersonic flight, moving closer to operational deployment of the RLV system.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of Space
prelimsPoints: Purpose of Reusable Launch Vehicles (RLVs).
ISRO's RLV-TD (Technology Demonstrator) project.
Benefits of reusable technology in space exploration (cost reduction, sustainability).
Key challenges in RLV development (materials, guidance, thermal protection).
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of developing Reusable Launch Vehicle technology for India's space program and its implications for global space competitiveness.
Analyze the potential economic and scientific benefits of reducing space launch costs through RLV technology.
Examine the technological challenges and innovations involved in designing and testing reusable launch vehicles.
Evaluate India's position in the global space race, highlighting its achievements and future aspirations in space technology.
articles.imageDescription: An artistic rendering of ISRO's Reusable Launch Vehicle prototype during a test flight.
articles.trivia: ISRO first successfully tested its RLV-TD in a winged flight experiment in 2016, demonstrating hypersonic flight and autonomous landing.

articles.headline: Monsoon Fury Leads to Multiple Fatal Road Accidents; Calls for Enhanced Safety Measures
articles.explainer:
• A series of tragic road accidents across India, including incidents in Modasa (Gujarat), Pune (Maharashtra), and Anuppur (Madhya Pradesh), have claimed multiple lives amid heavy monsoon rains.
• In Modasa, four teachers died when their car plunged into the Mazum River from a bridge, highlighting risks on poorly maintained infrastructure during adverse weather.
• Ten women were killed and 25 injured in Pune district after a pick-up van fell off a hilly road, emphasizing the dangers of overloaded vehicles and treacherous terrain.
• Five persons lost their lives in Anuppur following a jeep-motorcycle collision, underscoring the need for stricter adherence to traffic rules and improved road conditions.
• These incidents collectively bring renewed focus on road safety, disaster preparedness during monsoons, and the urgent need for infrastructure upgrades and public awareness campaigns.
gsSubTopics: Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
Disaster and disaster management
prelimsPoints: Factors contributing to road accidents in India (poor infrastructure, driver behavior, vehicle condition, weather).
Government initiatives for road safety (e.g., National Road Safety Council, Motor Vehicles Amendment Act, 2019).
Impact of monsoons on road conditions and accident rates.
Role of state disaster management authorities.
mainsPoints: Discuss the multi-faceted challenges in ensuring road safety in India, particularly during adverse weather conditions like monsoons.
Suggest comprehensive measures involving policy, infrastructure, enforcement, and public awareness to reduce road fatalities and injuries.
Analyze the socio-economic impact of road accidents on families and the national economy.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current disaster management strategies in mitigating risks associated with monsoon-related road hazards.
articles.imageDescription: A damaged vehicle on a waterlogged road during heavy rain, with emergency services in the background.
articles.trivia: India accounts for about 11% of the total global road accident deaths, despite having only 1% of the world's vehicles.

articles.headline: Tragic Incident in Patna Raises Alarms Over Food Safety and Public Health
articles.explainer:
• Three children from a family in Patna tragically died after allegedly consuming toxic food, prompting an urgent investigation by health authorities.
• The incident highlights persistent concerns regarding food safety standards, particularly in unorganized sectors and household food preparation.
• Authorities have initiated forensic analysis of food samples and are investigating the source of contamination, which could range from adulteration to improper storage or preparation.
• This tragedy underscores the critical need for greater public awareness about food hygiene, proper handling, and the dangers of consuming contaminated items.
• Calls for stricter enforcement of food safety regulations and regular inspections across the food supply chain have intensified following the incident.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
keyTerms: Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)
prelimsPoints: Role of FSSAI in ensuring food safety in India.
Key provisions of the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006.
Common causes of food contamination (microbial, chemical, physical).
Impact of foodborne diseases on public health and economy.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges in ensuring food safety across India, particularly in rural and unorganized urban settings, and suggest measures for improvement.
Analyze the role of regulatory bodies like FSSAI in enforcing food safety standards and protecting consumer health.
Examine the socio-economic factors that contribute to food adulteration and contamination in India.
Propose a multi-pronged strategy involving government, industry, and consumers to enhance food hygiene and prevent foodborne illnesses.
articles.imageDescription: A laboratory technician examining food samples for contaminants.
articles.trivia: FSSAI was established under the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, consolidating various acts and orders that had hitherto handled food-related issues in various Ministries and Departments.

articles.headline: Government Unveils Ambitious New Target for Green Hydrogen Production
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy today announced an ambitious new national target for Green Hydrogen production, aiming to establish India as a global hub for this clean fuel.
• The revised target seeks to significantly ramp up India's annual green hydrogen production capacity by 2030, aligning with the National Green Hydrogen Mission.
• This move is expected to attract substantial investments in renewable energy infrastructure, electrolyzer manufacturing, and associated supply chains.
• Green hydrogen, produced using renewable energy, is crucial for decarbonizing hard-to-abate sectors like steel, cement, and heavy transportation.
• The policy emphasizes incentives for domestic manufacturing, research & development, and pilot projects to foster a robust green hydrogen ecosystem.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Infrastructure: Energy.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
articles.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy
prelimsPoints: Definition of Green Hydrogen vs. Grey/Blue Hydrogen.
Objectives of India's National Green Hydrogen Mission.
Applications of Green Hydrogen (transport, industry, energy storage).
Role of renewable energy sources in green hydrogen production.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of Green Hydrogen for India's energy security and climate change mitigation goals.
Discuss the technological, economic, and infrastructure challenges in scaling up Green Hydrogen production in India.
Evaluate the potential of India to become a global hub for Green Hydrogen production and export, outlining necessary policy support.
Examine the interlinkages between renewable energy expansion and the success of the Green Hydrogen Mission.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized image showing a hydrogen molecule overlaid with a wind turbine and solar panels.
articles.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, making up roughly 75% of all normal matter.

articles.headline: Unified Farmer Service Platform Launched Under Digital India Initiative
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare today launched the 'Unified Farmer Service Platform' as part of the Digital India initiative, aiming to empower farmers with integrated digital services.
• The platform will provide a single window for farmers to access various government schemes, agricultural advisories, market information, and financial services.
• It integrates data from multiple sources, including land records, weather forecasts, crop patterns, and soil health cards, offering personalized recommendations.
• The initiative is expected to enhance transparency, reduce information asymmetry, and improve the efficiency of agricultural value chains.
• This digital push aims to modernize Indian agriculture, increase farmers' income, and ensure better access to resources and markets.
gsSubTopics: E-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations, and potential; citizens charters, transparency & accountability and institutional and other measures.

Major crops-cropping patterns in various parts of the country, - different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.
Technology missions; economics of animal-rearing.
prelimsPoints: Objectives of the Unified Farmer Service Platform.
Components and types of services offered (advisories, market data, schemes).
Role of e-governance in agriculture.
Other digital initiatives in agriculture (e.g., e-NAM, PM-KISAN).
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential of digital platforms and e-governance initiatives in transforming Indian agriculture and improving farmers' livelihoods.
Analyze the challenges in implementing large-scale digital solutions for farmers, considering issues like digital literacy and internet connectivity.
Evaluate how such unified platforms can address issues of market access, credit availability, and agricultural productivity.
Examine the role of data integration and personalized advisories in promoting sustainable agricultural practices.
articles.imageDescription: A farmer using a tablet to access digital agricultural services, with fields in the background.
articles.trivia: India has the second-largest agricultural land in the world, cultivating a diverse range of crops across various agro-climatic zones.

articles.headline: Government Reviews Forest Rights Act Implementation, Focus on Tribal Land Claims
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Tribal Affairs held a review meeting on the implementation of the Forest Rights Act (FRA), 2006, emphasizing the need to expedite the recognition of tribal land claims.
• The FRA aims to recognize and vest forest rights and occupation in forest dwelling Scheduled Tribes and other traditional forest dwellers who have been residing in such forests for generations.
• Concerns were raised regarding the slow pace of claim processing, rejection rates, and issues related to the recognition of Community Forest Resource (CFR) rights.
• The review highlighted the importance of strengthening the capacity of Gram Sabhas and district-level committees for effective implementation and grievance redressal.
• Improved implementation of FRA is crucial for tribal empowerment, sustainable forest management, and addressing historical injustices.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure.
prelimsPoints: Key provisions and objectives of the Forest Rights Act, 2006.
Role of Gram Sabha in FRA implementation.
Difference between Individual Forest Rights (IFR) and Community Forest Rights (CFR).
Ministry responsible for FRA implementation.
Overlap with PESA Act.
mainsPoints: Critically evaluate the implementation of the Forest Rights Act, 2006, in addressing historical injustices faced by forest-dwelling communities.
Discuss the challenges and bottlenecks in recognizing and vesting forest rights, and suggest measures to overcome them.
Analyze the significance of Community Forest Resource (CFR) rights for sustainable forest management and tribal self-governance.
Examine the socio-economic and environmental impacts of both successful and delayed FRA implementation.
articles.imageDescription: A group of tribal villagers gathered in front of a forest, symbolizing community rights.
articles.trivia: The Forest Rights Act, 2006, is considered a landmark legislation as it recognizes the symbiotic relationship between forest-dwelling communities and forests.

articles.headline: RBI Releases Quarterly Inflation Report; Outlook Indicates Moderating Pressures
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) today released its quarterly Inflation Report, providing an updated assessment of price stability and economic outlook.
• The report indicated a moderating trend in headline inflation, primarily driven by a softening of food prices and stable core inflation.
• The RBI maintained its stance on carefully monitoring global commodity prices and domestic demand pressures that could influence future inflation trajectory.
• Projections for the upcoming quarters suggest inflation is likely to remain within the tolerance band, though risks from volatile crude oil prices and erratic monsoons persist.
• The report's findings will be crucial for the Monetary Policy Committee's (MPC) future decisions on interest rates and liquidity management.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Monetary policy.
prelimsPoints: Functions of the RBI (monetary policy, inflation targeting).
Role and composition of the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC).
Difference between CPI and WPI.
Factors influencing inflation (demand-pull, cost-push, global factors).
RBI's inflation target band.
mainsPoints: Analyze the current inflation trends in India and the factors contributing to their moderation or persistence.
Discuss the tools and strategies employed by the RBI's Monetary Policy Committee to manage inflation and maintain price stability.
Examine the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control with economic growth objectives.
Evaluate the impact of global economic developments and domestic agricultural output on India's inflation trajectory.
articles.imageDescription: A graph showing a downward trend in inflation, with the RBI logo in the background.
articles.trivia: The Reserve Bank of India was established on April 1, 1935, in accordance with the provisions of the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.

articles.headline: Parliamentary Panel Recommends Sweeping Reforms in Police Training and Modernization
articles.explainer:
• A Parliamentary Standing Committee today submitted its report recommending comprehensive reforms in police training, infrastructure, and modernization across India.
• The report highlighted deficiencies in existing training modules, lack of specialized skills, and inadequate use of technology in policing.
• Key recommendations include updating training curricula to incorporate cybercrime, forensic science, community policing, and soft skills.
• The panel also advocated for increased budgetary allocation for police modernization, better living conditions for personnel, and a focus on psychological well-being.
• These reforms are deemed essential to enhance police effectiveness, improve public perception, and address contemporary law and order challenges.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Role of civil services in a democracy.
prelimsPoints: Key recommendations for police reforms in India.
Challenges faced by Indian police forces.
Concept of 'Smart Policing'.
Role of parliamentary committees in governance.
mainsPoints: Discuss the persistent challenges faced by the Indian police force and the necessity of comprehensive police reforms in a democratic setup.
Analyze the key recommendations of various committees on police reforms, including those related to training, modernization, and accountability.
Examine the role of technology and community policing in enhancing police effectiveness and improving public trust.
Evaluate the constitutional and administrative hurdles in implementing police reforms in India's federal structure.
articles.imageDescription: Police cadets undergoing training exercises at a police academy.
articles.trivia: The Police Act of 1861, enacted during British rule, still forms the basis of policing in India, often criticized for its colonial legacy.

articles.headline: New Study Highlights Increasing Coastal Erosion Threats to Indian Shorelines
articles.explainer:
• A new scientific study published today warns of increasing coastal erosion along significant stretches of India's shoreline, posing a severe threat to ecosystems and coastal communities.
• The study, using satellite imagery and ground surveys, identified several highly vulnerable regions, particularly in states like West Bengal, Odisha, Kerala, and Gujarat.
• Factors contributing to accelerated erosion include rising sea levels, frequent extreme weather events, anthropogenic activities like sand mining, and inadequate coastal zone management.
• The loss of mangroves, coral reefs, and other natural coastal defenses further exacerbates the problem, impacting biodiversity and livelihoods.
• Experts call for urgent implementation of integrated coastal zone management plans, nature-based solutions, and relocation strategies for at-risk populations.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.
Changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
prelimsPoints: Major causes of coastal erosion in India.
Vulnerable Indian states/regions to coastal erosion.
Impacts of coastal erosion (environmental, economic, social).
Role of mangroves and coral reefs in coastal protection.
Concept of Integrated Coastal Zone Management (ICZM).
mainsPoints: Analyze the drivers and impacts of coastal erosion on India's diverse coastline, considering both natural and anthropogenic factors.
Discuss the challenges in implementing effective coastal zone management strategies and suggest integrated approaches to mitigate erosion.
Evaluate the role of nature-based solutions (e.g., mangrove restoration) in enhancing coastal resilience against climate change impacts.
Examine the socio-economic implications of coastal erosion for fishing communities and coastal infrastructure.
articles.imageDescription: An aerial view of a coastline showing significant land loss due to erosion, with waves crashing.
articles.trivia: India has a coastline of about 7,516.6 km, including the mainland and island territories, making it highly susceptible to coastal processes.

articles.headline: Ministry of Health Launches Nationwide Mental Health Awareness Campaign
articles.explainer:
• The Union Ministry of Health and Family Welfare today launched a nationwide campaign to raise awareness about mental health, aiming to destigmatize mental illness and promote well-being.
• The campaign focuses on early identification of mental health issues, encouraging help-seeking behavior, and providing accessible resources.
• It will utilize a multi-media approach, including digital platforms, community outreach programs, and collaborations with healthcare providers and NGOs.
• A key objective is to educate the public on common mental health conditions like depression, anxiety, and stress, and emphasize that mental health is as important as physical health.
• The initiative aligns with the National Mental Health Program and targets vulnerable groups, including youth, women, and rural populations.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: Objectives of the Mental Health Awareness Campaign.
Key provisions of the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017.
National Mental Health Program (NMHP).
Common mental health conditions.
Government initiatives like Tele-MANAS.
mainsPoints: Discuss the growing burden of mental health issues in India and the societal stigma associated with mental illness.
Analyze the effectiveness of government initiatives and awareness campaigns in addressing mental health challenges and promoting well-being.
Examine the gaps in mental healthcare infrastructure and human resources in India, particularly in rural areas, and suggest policy interventions.
Evaluate the role of community participation and digital platforms in expanding access to mental health services and support.
articles.imageDescription: A diverse group of people engaged in a discussion, symbolizing mental health support and community.
articles.trivia: The Mental Healthcare Act, 2017, decriminalized suicide attempts and ensured the right to access mental healthcare for all citizens.

articles.headline: India Hosts International Yoga Festival, Reinforcing Cultural Diplomacy
articles.explainer:
• India today hosted the inaugural 'International Yoga Festival' in a major city, bringing together practitioners, gurus, and enthusiasts from over 50 countries.
• The festival featured workshops, meditation sessions, academic discussions on yoga's philosophy, and cultural performances, promoting India's ancient heritage.
• This event serves as a significant exercise in cultural diplomacy, showcasing India's soft power and its contributions to global well-being.
• The initiative aims to further popularize yoga worldwide, reinforcing its benefits for physical and mental health, and fostering international understanding.
• Dignitaries highlighted yoga's role in promoting peace, harmony, and a holistic lifestyle in an increasingly interconnected world.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
keyTerms: Ayurveda, Yoga & Naturopathy, Unani, Siddha and Homoeopathy (AYUSH)
prelimsPoints: Concept of cultural diplomacy and soft power.
Origin and global spread of Yoga.
International Day of Yoga (June 21).
Role of AYUSH Ministry.
mainsPoints: Discuss the growing importance of cultural diplomacy in India's foreign policy and how initiatives like the International Yoga Festival contribute to it.
Analyze the role of yoga as a soft power tool for India in fostering international goodwill and promoting its cultural heritage.
Examine the global appeal of traditional Indian practices like yoga and Ayurveda in promoting health and well-being.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities in leveraging cultural assets for national interest on the global stage.
articles.imageDescription: People performing yoga poses in an open-air setting with a diverse crowd.
articles.trivia: The proposal for an International Day of Yoga was first introduced by India at the United Nations General Assembly in 2014, and it was adopted by consensus.

articles.headline: India and Sri Lanka Hold Bilateral Talks on Enhancing Maritime Security Cooperation
articles.explainer:
• India and Sri Lanka held high-level bilateral discussions today focusing on strengthening maritime security cooperation in the Indian Ocean region.
• The talks covered joint patrols, intelligence sharing, capacity building for naval and coast guard forces, and combating transnational crimes like drug trafficking and illegal fishing.
• Both nations reiterated their commitment to ensuring peace and stability in the Indian Ocean, a critical maritime trade route.
• The discussions also touched upon humanitarian assistance and disaster relief (HADR) operations, enhancing interoperability between their forces.
• This engagement is part of India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy and its broader vision for Security and Growth for All in the Region (SAGAR).
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
prelimsPoints: India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy and SAGAR doctrine.
Key areas of India-Sri Lanka cooperation (maritime security, trade, cultural).
Importance of the Indian Ocean for India's security and economy.
Challenges in maritime security (piracy, drug trafficking, IUU fishing).
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of Sri Lanka for India's maritime security and regional stability in the Indian Ocean.
Discuss the various facets of India-Sri Lanka cooperation in maritime security, including joint exercises and intelligence sharing.
Examine the challenges in bilateral relations, particularly concerning issues like illegal fishing and external influences.
Evaluate the effectiveness of India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy in building stronger ties with its maritime neighbors.
articles.imageDescription: Naval ships of India and Sri Lanka conducting a joint exercise in the Indian Ocean.
articles.trivia: The Palk Strait, a narrow strip of water separating India and Sri Lanka, is a traditional fishing ground, often leading to disputes.

articles.headline: Government Releases Draft Framework for Ethical AI Development and Deployment
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) today released a draft framework for the ethical development and deployment of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in India.
• The framework aims to ensure that AI systems are fair, transparent, accountable, secure, and respectful of privacy and human rights.
• It proposes guidelines for data governance, algorithmic bias detection, human oversight, and mechanisms for redressal in case of AI-related harm.
• Public consultation has been invited on the draft, seeking inputs from industry experts, academia, civil society, and other stakeholders.
• This move signals India's commitment to fostering responsible AI innovation while mitigating potential risks associated with advanced AI technologies.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: Key principles of ethical AI (fairness, transparency, accountability, privacy).
Challenges of AI (bias, privacy, job displacement).
Government bodies involved in AI policy (MeitY, NITI Aayog).
Related legislation like the Digital Personal Data Protection Act.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ethical challenges and societal implications arising from the rapid development and deployment of Artificial Intelligence in India.
Analyze the key features of the proposed ethical AI framework and its potential to ensure responsible AI innovation.
Examine the balance between fostering AI innovation and mitigating risks such as algorithmic bias, job displacement, and privacy concerns.
Evaluate the role of government, industry, and civil society in building a trustworthy and human-centric AI ecosystem in India.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized image of a human hand shaking a robotic hand, with lines representing data flow, symbolizing AI ethics.
articles.trivia: India is one of the fastest-growing AI markets globally, with significant applications in healthcare, finance, and e-governance.

articles.headline: Study Reveals Intensifying Urban Heat Island Effect in Major Indian Cities
articles.explainer:
• A new environmental study published today highlights a significant intensification of the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect in several major Indian cities, leading to higher temperatures than surrounding rural areas.
• The research attributes this phenomenon to rapid urbanization, extensive concrete surfaces, reduced green cover, and increased anthropogenic heat emissions.
• Cities like Delhi, Ahmedabad, and Hyderabad showed particularly pronounced UHI effects, with nighttime temperatures significantly elevated.
• The intensified UHI poses serious public health risks, including heat stress and respiratory illnesses, and increases energy demand for cooling.
• Experts recommend urgent implementation of urban planning strategies such as increasing green spaces, using cool pavements, and promoting sustainable building materials to mitigate the UHI effect.
gsSubTopics: Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
Geographical features and their location- changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
Environmental pollution and degradation.
prelimsPoints: Definition and causes of the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect.
Impacts of UHI (health, energy consumption, environmental).
Mitigation strategies for UHI (green roofs, cool pavements, urban forests).
Role of urbanization and climate change in intensifying UHI.
mainsPoints: Discuss the causes and consequences of the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect in rapidly urbanizing Indian cities.
Analyze the public health and environmental challenges posed by intensifying UHI, particularly in the context of climate change.
Suggest sustainable urban planning and architectural solutions to mitigate the UHI effect and enhance urban resilience.
Examine the role of local bodies and citizen participation in promoting green infrastructure and reducing heat stress in cities.
articles.imageDescription: An infrared map of a city showing warmer urban areas (red) compared to cooler rural areas (blue).
articles.trivia: The Urban Heat Island effect can make city temperatures several degrees Celsius warmer than surrounding rural areas, especially at night.

articles.headline: Parliamentary Session Sees Robust Debate on Key Legislations; Focus on Productivity
articles.explainer:
• The ongoing parliamentary session witnessed robust debates on several key legislations, with both ruling and opposition parties engaging in discussions on national importance.
• Productivity of the Houses remained a central theme, with efforts to maximize legislative business and minimize disruptions.
• Bills related to economic reforms, social welfare, and environmental protection were introduced and deliberated upon, highlighting the legislative agenda.
• While some contentious issues led to temporary stalemates, overall efforts were made to ensure constructive parliamentary proceedings.
• The session underscored the importance of parliamentary democracy in India, serving as a platform for accountability and policy formulation.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Parliament and State Legislatures—structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these.
prelimsPoints: Functions of Parliament (legislative, oversight, deliberative).
Types of parliamentary sessions (Budget, Monsoon, Winter).
Mechanisms for legislative debate (bills, motions, resolutions).
Importance of parliamentary productivity.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of parliamentary debates in a democratic system and the challenges to ensuring productive legislative sessions in India.
Analyze the role of both ruling and opposition parties in shaping legislative outcomes and holding the executive accountable.
Examine the impact of disruptions and procedural issues on parliamentary functioning and suggest measures to enhance efficiency.
Evaluate the importance of robust legislative processes in strengthening India's democratic institutions and policy-making.
articles.imageDescription: The interior of the Indian Parliament building during a session, with members debating.
articles.trivia: The Indian Parliament consists of the President, the Lok Sabha (House of the People), and the Rajya Sabha (Council of States).

highlights: Supreme Court modifies order on Delhi stray dogs, emphasizing comprehensive management with sterilization and immunization.
Nation commemorates the 83rd anniversary of the Quit India Movement, remembering its pivotal role in the freedom struggle.
Union Cabinet approves new National Logistics Policy, aiming to significantly reduce logistics costs and boost economic competitiveness.
ISRO successfully tests a crucial prototype for its Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV), advancing India's cost-effective space access.
Tragic road accidents in Gujarat, Maharashtra, and MP, alongside a food poisoning incident in Patna, highlight urgent public safety concerns.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the National Logistics Policy (NLP), consider the following statements:
1. The primary objective of NLP is to increase India's logistics cost as a percentage of GDP to improve quality.
2. The policy aims to integrate various modes of transport and leverage technology for efficient goods movement.
3. The PM Gati Shakti National Master Plan is an unrelated initiative focused solely on infrastructure development.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect. The primary objective of NLP is to *reduce* India's logistics cost as a percentage of GDP, not increase it. Statement 2 is correct, as the policy seeks to integrate transport modes and use technology. Statement 3 is incorrect. The PM Gati Shakti National Master Plan is a related initiative, designed to break departmental silos and integrate planning and implementation of infrastructure projects, which directly supports the objectives of the NLP.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following statements about the Quit India Movement are correct?
1. It was launched by Mahatma Gandhi in 1942.
2. The movement called for complete independence with the slogan 'Do or Die'.
3. Major leaders were arrested immediately after its launch.
4. It was formally endorsed by the Muslim League.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statements 1, 2, and 3 are correct regarding the Quit India Movement. Mahatma Gandhi launched it in 1942, the 'Do or Die' slogan was central, and most prominent leaders were arrested immediately. Statement 4 is incorrect; the Muslim League did not endorse the Quit India Movement and instead passed a resolution to 'divide and quit' in 1943.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following about Green Hydrogen:
1. It is produced using renewable energy sources through electrolysis.
2. It is primarily used for domestic cooking and lighting.
3. India aims to become a global hub for its production by 2030.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct; Green Hydrogen is produced by splitting water using electricity generated from renewable sources. Statement 2 is incorrect; while hydrogen can be used for energy, green hydrogen's primary strategic use is for decarbonizing 'hard-to-abate' industrial sectors like steel, cement, and heavy transport, not typically domestic cooking and lighting. Statement 3 is correct; India's National Green Hydrogen Mission aims to establish the country as a global hub for green hydrogen production and export by 2030.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following are potential impacts of the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect?
1. Increased energy demand for cooling.
2. Exacerbation of heat-related illnesses.
3. Reduction in air pollution levels.
4. Decreased runoff from precipitation.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statements 1 and 2 are correct. UHI leads to higher temperatures, increasing the need for air conditioning and thus energy demand, and also exacerbates heat stress and related illnesses. Statement 3 is incorrect; higher temperatures in UHIs can increase the formation of ground-level ozone and other pollutants, potentially *increasing* air pollution. Statement 4 is incorrect; extensive impervious surfaces (concrete, asphalt) in urban areas, which contribute to UHI, actually *increase* stormwater runoff, rather than decrease it.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the Forest Rights Act (FRA), 2006, consider the following statements:
1. It recognizes and vests forest rights in Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers.
2. The Gram Sabha plays a crucial role in initiating and approving the process for recognizing individual and community forest rights.
3. The Act primarily focuses on individual forest rights and does not include provisions for Community Forest Resource (CFR) rights.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct; the FRA's core objective is to recognize the rights of forest-dwelling communities. Statement 2 is correct; the Gram Sabha is the primary authority for initiating the process of determining the nature and extent of individual and community forest rights. Statement 3 is incorrect; the FRA explicitly includes provisions for both Individual Forest Rights (IFR) and Community Forest Resource (CFR) rights, recognizing traditional collective rights over forest lands.

mainsMocks.question: Critically analyze the Supreme Court's evolving stance on stray animal management in urban areas. Discuss the challenges in balancing public safety, animal welfare, and effective policy implementation by local governments.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation; Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population; Judiciary.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the SC ruling on stray dogs, highlighting the balance sought. Discuss challenges like rabies, dog bites, public fear vs. animal rights, humane treatment. Analyze policy implementation issues: resource constraints, lack of coordination, community participation. Conclude with a balanced approach involving judicial oversight, community involvement, and robust government programs.

mainsMocks.question: The approval of the National Logistics Policy (NLP) is seen as a game-changer for India's economic competitiveness. Discuss the key objectives and potential impacts of the NLP on India's manufacturing, agriculture, and trade sectors. What are the major challenges in its effective implementation?
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment; Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.; Investment models.
mainsMocks.approach: Start with the NLP's aim to reduce logistics costs. Detail objectives like multimodal integration, technology adoption, and improved efficiency. Explain impacts on manufacturing (reduced costs, faster supply chains), agriculture (better market access, less wastage), and trade (enhanced competitiveness, easier exports). Conclude by discussing implementation challenges such as infrastructure gaps, regulatory hurdles, digital literacy, and inter-ministerial coordination.

mainsMocks.question: India's push for Green Hydrogen is crucial for its energy security and climate goals. Examine the strategic importance of Green Hydrogen for India. What technological and economic hurdles must be overcome to scale up its production and establish India as a global leader in this sector?
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment; Infrastructure: Energy; Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce Green Hydrogen as a clean fuel. Discuss its strategic importance for energy independence, decarbonization of heavy industries, and meeting climate targets. Detail technological hurdles (electrolyzer efficiency, storage, transport) and economic hurdles (high production cost, infrastructure investment, market development). Suggest policy measures, R&D, and international collaborations for India to achieve leadership.

quickBytes.text: August 9 is observed as the International Day of the World's Indigenous Peoples by the UN.
The 'Do or Die' slogan was coined by Mahatma Gandhi during the Quit India Movement.
India's logistics cost is currently estimated at 13-14% of its GDP.
The Animal Birth Control (Dogs) Rules, 2001, govern the sterilization and immunization of stray dogs in India.
ISRO's Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) aims to significantly reduce the cost of space missions.
The Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) is the apex body for food safety regulations.
Green Hydrogen is produced using renewable energy, making it a zero-carbon fuel.
The Forest Rights Act (FRA), 2006, recognizes rights of forest dwellers over forest land and resources.
The Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect causes urban areas to be significantly warmer than surrounding rural areas.
India and Japan are key partners in promoting a 'Free and Open Indo-Pacific'.

GS1.headline: Nation Commemorates 83rd Anniversary of Quit India Movement
GS1.explainer:
• August 9, 2025, marks the 83rd anniversary of the Quit India Movement, a pivotal moment in India's struggle for independence against British colonial rule.
• Launched by Mahatma Gandhi in 1942, the movement called for an immediate end to British rule, with the slogan 'Do or Die'.
• The movement saw widespread civil disobedience, protests, and strikes across the country, despite the immediate arrest of major leaders.
• It galvanized the Indian masses and demonstrated their unwavering resolve for complete independence, significantly influencing the eventual British withdrawal.
• Various commemorative events, seminars, and pledges were held across the nation to remember the sacrifices of freedom fighters.
gsSubTopics: Modern Indian history from about the middle of the eighteenth century until the present- significant events, personalities, issues
The Freedom Struggle - its various stages and important contributors /contributions from different parts of the country
prelimsPoints: Date of Quit India Movement launch (August 8, 1942, resolution passed; movement started Aug 9).
Key leaders associated with the movement (Gandhi, Nehru, Patel, Azad, Aruna Asaf Ali, Jayaprakash Narayan).
Slogan 'Do or Die'.
Reasons for its launch (failure of Cripps Mission, WWII impact, growing impatience).
Impact of the movement despite leadership arrests.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the Quit India Movement in accelerating India's journey to independence.
Discuss the nature and extent of mass participation in the Quit India Movement, highlighting its strengths and weaknesses.
Evaluate the role of Mahatma Gandhi's leadership and the 'Do or Die' call in shaping the trajectory of the movement.
Compare and contrast the Quit India Movement with earlier phases of the freedom struggle.
GS1.imageDescription: A historical photograph of a large crowd demonstrating during the Quit India Movement.
GS1.trivia: The 'Quit India' slogan itself was coined by Yusuf Meherally, a socialist and trade unionist, who also coined the slogan 'Simon Go Back'.

GS1.headline: Monsoon Fury Leads to Multiple Fatal Road Accidents; Calls for Enhanced Safety Measures
GS1.explainer:
• A series of tragic road accidents across India, including incidents in Modasa (Gujarat), Pune (Maharashtra), and Anuppur (Madhya Pradesh), have claimed multiple lives amid heavy monsoon rains.
• In Modasa, four teachers died when their car plunged into the Mazum River from a bridge, highlighting risks on poorly maintained infrastructure during adverse weather.
• Ten women were killed and 25 injured in Pune district after a pick-up van fell off a hilly road, emphasizing the dangers of overloaded vehicles and treacherous terrain.
• Five persons lost their lives in Anuppur following a jeep-motorcycle collision, underscoring the need for stricter adherence to traffic rules and improved road conditions.
• These incidents collectively bring renewed focus on road safety, disaster preparedness during monsoons, and the urgent need for infrastructure upgrades and public awareness campaigns.
gsSubTopics: Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
Disaster and disaster management
prelimsPoints: Factors contributing to road accidents in India (poor infrastructure, driver behavior, vehicle condition, weather).
Government initiatives for road safety (e.g., National Road Safety Council, Motor Vehicles Amendment Act, 2019).
Impact of monsoons on road conditions and accident rates.
Role of state disaster management authorities.
mainsPoints: Discuss the multi-faceted challenges in ensuring road safety in India, particularly during adverse weather conditions like monsoons.
Suggest comprehensive measures involving policy, infrastructure, enforcement, and public awareness to reduce road fatalities and injuries.
Analyze the socio-economic impact of road accidents on families and the national economy.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current disaster management strategies in mitigating risks associated with monsoon-related road hazards.
GS1.imageDescription: A damaged vehicle on a waterlogged road during heavy rain, with emergency services in the background.
GS1.trivia: India accounts for about 11% of the total global road accident deaths, despite having only 1% of the world's vehicles.

GS1.headline: Study Reveals Intensifying Urban Heat Island Effect in Major Indian Cities
GS1.explainer:
• A new environmental study published today highlights a significant intensification of the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect in several major Indian cities, leading to higher temperatures than surrounding rural areas.
• The research attributes this phenomenon to rapid urbanization, extensive concrete surfaces, reduced green cover, and increased anthropogenic heat emissions.
• Cities like Delhi, Ahmedabad, and Hyderabad showed particularly pronounced UHI effects, with nighttime temperatures significantly elevated.
• The intensified UHI poses serious public health risks, including heat stress and respiratory illnesses, and increases energy demand for cooling.
• Experts recommend urgent implementation of urban planning strategies such as increasing green spaces, using cool pavements, and promoting sustainable building materials to mitigate the UHI effect.
gsSubTopics: Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
Geographical features and their location- changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
Environmental pollution and degradation.
prelimsPoints: Definition and causes of the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect.
Impacts of UHI (health, energy consumption, environmental).
Mitigation strategies for UHI (green roofs, cool pavements, urban forests).
Role of urbanization and climate change in intensifying UHI.
mainsPoints: Discuss the causes and consequences of the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect in rapidly urbanizing Indian cities.
Analyze the public health and environmental challenges posed by intensifying UHI, particularly in the context of climate change.
Suggest sustainable urban planning and architectural solutions to mitigate the UHI effect and enhance urban resilience.
Examine the role of local bodies and citizen participation in promoting green infrastructure and reducing heat stress in cities.
GS1.imageDescription: An infrared map of a city showing warmer urban areas (red) compared to cooler rural areas (blue).
GS1.trivia: The Urban Heat Island effect can make city temperatures several degrees Celsius warmer than surrounding rural areas, especially at night.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Directs Delhi Government on Comprehensive Stray Dog Management Plan
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, on an appeal, modified its earlier order, directing the Delhi government to implement a comprehensive plan for managing stray dogs, allowing for the release of sterilized and immunized dogs.
• The initial order had called for all stray dogs to be transferred to animal shelters within eight weeks, citing public safety concerns due to dog bites and rabies.
• The modified directive emphasizes a balanced approach, prioritizing public health and safety while also ensuring humane treatment and welfare of animals.
• Key components of the revised plan include mandatory sterilization, immunization, identification, and a robust adoption program, alongside community engagement initiatives.
• This ruling comes amidst increasing incidents of dog bites and concerns from both residents and animal welfare organizations regarding the handling of stray populations.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population
Role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders
Judiciary
prelimsPoints: Supreme Court's role in public health and welfare issues.
Provisions related to animal welfare in India (e.g., Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960).
Animal Birth Control (Dogs) Rules, 2001 and subsequent updates.
Jurisdiction of state governments vs. central guidelines on animal management.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges of managing stray animal populations in urban areas, balancing public safety with animal rights.
Analyze the role of the judiciary in directing policy implementation and ensuring accountability of state governments in welfare issues.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current animal birth control programs and suggest measures for improvement.
Examine the ethical considerations and legal framework surrounding animal welfare in India.
GS2.imageDescription: A graphic illustrating a dog being vaccinated, with the Supreme Court building in the background.
GS2.trivia: The Animal Welfare Board of India (AWBI) was established in 1962 under Section 4 of The Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960, making it the first of its kind in the world.

GS2.headline: India-Japan Strategic Dialogue Concludes, Emphasizes Indo-Pacific Cooperation and Economic Ties
GS2.explainer:
• The annual India-Japan Strategic Dialogue concluded today, reaffirming both nations' commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific region.
• Discussions focused on enhancing defense cooperation, maritime security, economic partnerships, and collaboration in emerging technologies.
• Both sides stressed the importance of upholding international law, particularly UNCLOS, and ensuring freedom of navigation and overflight in critical sea lanes.
• New initiatives were announced to boost investment, promote clean energy projects, and strengthen supply chain resilience between the two countries.
• The dialogue underscored the growing strategic alignment between India and Japan as key pillars of regional stability and economic growth.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: Key areas of cooperation between India and Japan (defense, economy, technology, maritime).
Importance of Indo-Pacific concept for both nations.
UNCLOS (United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea).
Related groupings like Quad (Quadrilateral Security Dialogue).
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolution and significance of the India-Japan Special Strategic and Global Partnership in the context of changing geopolitical dynamics in the Indo-Pacific.
Discuss how India and Japan are collaborating to ensure a stable and prosperous Indo-Pacific region, addressing common security challenges.
Examine the economic dimensions of India-Japan relations, including trade, investment, and infrastructure development.
Evaluate the role of bilateral and multilateral frameworks (e.g., Quad) in shaping India's foreign policy towards East Asia.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and Japan displayed during a diplomatic meeting.
GS2.trivia: Japan is a significant investor in India's infrastructure, notably contributing to the Mumbai-Ahmedabad High-Speed Rail project.

GS2.headline: Tragic Incident in Patna Raises Alarms Over Food Safety and Public Health
GS2.explainer:
• Three children from a family in Patna tragically died after allegedly consuming toxic food, prompting an urgent investigation by health authorities.
• The incident highlights persistent concerns regarding food safety standards, particularly in unorganized sectors and household food preparation.
• Authorities have initiated forensic analysis of food samples and are investigating the source of contamination, which could range from adulteration to improper storage or preparation.
• This tragedy underscores the critical need for greater public awareness about food hygiene, proper handling, and the dangers of consuming contaminated items.
• Calls for stricter enforcement of food safety regulations and regular inspections across the food supply chain have intensified following the incident.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
keyTerms: Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)
prelimsPoints: Role of FSSAI in ensuring food safety in India.
Key provisions of the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006.
Common causes of food contamination (microbial, chemical, physical).
Impact of foodborne diseases on public health and economy.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges in ensuring food safety across India, particularly in rural and unorganized urban settings, and suggest measures for improvement.
Analyze the role of regulatory bodies like FSSAI in enforcing food safety standards and protecting consumer health.
Examine the socio-economic factors that contribute to food adulteration and contamination in India.
Propose a multi-pronged strategy involving government, industry, and consumers to enhance food hygiene and prevent foodborne illnesses.
GS2.imageDescription: A laboratory technician examining food samples for contaminants.
GS2.trivia: FSSAI was established under the Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, consolidating various acts and orders that had hitherto handled food-related issues in various Ministries and Departments.

GS2.headline: Government Reviews Forest Rights Act Implementation, Focus on Tribal Land Claims
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Tribal Affairs held a review meeting on the implementation of the Forest Rights Act (FRA), 2006, emphasizing the need to expedite the recognition of tribal land claims.
• The FRA aims to recognize and vest forest rights and occupation in forest dwelling Scheduled Tribes and other traditional forest dwellers who have been residing in such forests for generations.
• Concerns were raised regarding the slow pace of claim processing, rejection rates, and issues related to the recognition of Community Forest Resource (CFR) rights.
• The review highlighted the importance of strengthening the capacity of Gram Sabhas and district-level committees for effective implementation and grievance redressal.
• Improved implementation of FRA is crucial for tribal empowerment, sustainable forest management, and addressing historical injustices.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure.
prelimsPoints: Key provisions and objectives of the Forest Rights Act, 2006.
Role of Gram Sabha in FRA implementation.
Difference between Individual Forest Rights (IFR) and Community Forest Rights (CFR).
Ministry responsible for FRA implementation.
Overlap with PESA Act.
mainsPoints: Critically evaluate the implementation of the Forest Rights Act, 2006, in addressing historical injustices faced by forest-dwelling communities.
Discuss the challenges and bottlenecks in recognizing and vesting forest rights, and suggest measures to overcome them.
Analyze the significance of Community Forest Resource (CFR) rights for sustainable forest management and tribal self-governance.
Examine the socio-economic and environmental impacts of both successful and delayed FRA implementation.
GS2.imageDescription: A group of tribal villagers gathered in front of a forest, symbolizing community rights.
GS2.trivia: The Forest Rights Act, 2006, is considered a landmark legislation as it recognizes the symbiotic relationship between forest-dwelling communities and forests.

GS2.headline: Parliamentary Panel Recommends Sweeping Reforms in Police Training and Modernization
GS2.explainer:
• A Parliamentary Standing Committee today submitted its report recommending comprehensive reforms in police training, infrastructure, and modernization across India.
• The report highlighted deficiencies in existing training modules, lack of specialized skills, and inadequate use of technology in policing.
• Key recommendations include updating training curricula to incorporate cybercrime, forensic science, community policing, and soft skills.
• The panel also advocated for increased budgetary allocation for police modernization, better living conditions for personnel, and a focus on psychological well-being.
• These reforms are deemed essential to enhance police effectiveness, improve public perception, and address contemporary law and order challenges.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Role of civil services in a democracy.
prelimsPoints: Key recommendations for police reforms in India.
Challenges faced by Indian police forces.
Concept of 'Smart Policing'.
Role of parliamentary committees in governance.
mainsPoints: Discuss the persistent challenges faced by the Indian police force and the necessity of comprehensive police reforms in a democratic setup.
Analyze the key recommendations of various committees on police reforms, including those related to training, modernization, and accountability.
Examine the role of technology and community policing in enhancing police effectiveness and improving public trust.
Evaluate the constitutional and administrative hurdles in implementing police reforms in India's federal structure.
GS2.imageDescription: Police cadets undergoing training exercises at a police academy.
GS2.trivia: The Police Act of 1861, enacted during British rule, still forms the basis of policing in India, often criticized for its colonial legacy.

GS2.headline: Ministry of Health Launches Nationwide Mental Health Awareness Campaign
GS2.explainer:
• The Union Ministry of Health and Family Welfare today launched a nationwide campaign to raise awareness about mental health, aiming to destigmatize mental illness and promote well-being.
• The campaign focuses on early identification of mental health issues, encouraging help-seeking behavior, and providing accessible resources.
• It will utilize a multi-media approach, including digital platforms, community outreach programs, and collaborations with healthcare providers and NGOs.
• A key objective is to educate the public on common mental health conditions like depression, anxiety, and stress, and emphasize that mental health is as important as physical health.
• The initiative aligns with the National Mental Health Program and targets vulnerable groups, including youth, women, and rural populations.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: Objectives of the Mental Health Awareness Campaign.
Key provisions of the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017.
National Mental Health Program (NMHP).
Common mental health conditions.
Government initiatives like Tele-MANAS.
mainsPoints: Discuss the growing burden of mental health issues in India and the societal stigma associated with mental illness.
Analyze the effectiveness of government initiatives and awareness campaigns in addressing mental health challenges and promoting well-being.
Examine the gaps in mental healthcare infrastructure and human resources in India, particularly in rural areas, and suggest policy interventions.
Evaluate the role of community participation and digital platforms in expanding access to mental health services and support.
GS2.imageDescription: A diverse group of people engaged in a discussion, symbolizing mental health support and community.
GS2.trivia: The Mental Healthcare Act, 2017, decriminalized suicide attempts and ensured the right to access mental healthcare for all citizens.

GS2.headline: India Hosts International Yoga Festival, Reinforcing Cultural Diplomacy
GS2.explainer:
• India today hosted the inaugural 'International Yoga Festival' in a major city, bringing together practitioners, gurus, and enthusiasts from over 50 countries.
• The festival featured workshops, meditation sessions, academic discussions on yoga's philosophy, and cultural performances, promoting India's ancient heritage.
• This event serves as a significant exercise in cultural diplomacy, showcasing India's soft power and its contributions to global well-being.
• The initiative aims to further popularize yoga worldwide, reinforcing its benefits for physical and mental health, and fostering international understanding.
• Dignitaries highlighted yoga's role in promoting peace, harmony, and a holistic lifestyle in an increasingly interconnected world.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
keyTerms: Ayurveda, Yoga & Naturopathy, Unani, Siddha and Homoeopathy (AYUSH)
prelimsPoints: Concept of cultural diplomacy and soft power.
Origin and global spread of Yoga.
International Day of Yoga (June 21).
Role of AYUSH Ministry.
mainsPoints: Discuss the growing importance of cultural diplomacy in India's foreign policy and how initiatives like the International Yoga Festival contribute to it.
Analyze the role of yoga as a soft power tool for India in fostering international goodwill and promoting its cultural heritage.
Examine the global appeal of traditional Indian practices like yoga and Ayurveda in promoting health and well-being.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities in leveraging cultural assets for national interest on the global stage.
GS2.imageDescription: People performing yoga poses in an open-air setting with a diverse crowd.
GS2.trivia: The proposal for an International Day of Yoga was first introduced by India at the United Nations General Assembly in 2014, and it was adopted by consensus.

GS2.headline: India and Sri Lanka Hold Bilateral Talks on Enhancing Maritime Security Cooperation
GS2.explainer:
• India and Sri Lanka held high-level bilateral discussions today focusing on strengthening maritime security cooperation in the Indian Ocean region.
• The talks covered joint patrols, intelligence sharing, capacity building for naval and coast guard forces, and combating transnational crimes like drug trafficking and illegal fishing.
• Both nations reiterated their commitment to ensuring peace and stability in the Indian Ocean, a critical maritime trade route.
• The discussions also touched upon humanitarian assistance and disaster relief (HADR) operations, enhancing interoperability between their forces.
• This engagement is part of India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy and its broader vision for Security and Growth for All in the Region (SAGAR).
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
prelimsPoints: India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy and SAGAR doctrine.
Key areas of India-Sri Lanka cooperation (maritime security, trade, cultural).
Importance of the Indian Ocean for India's security and economy.
Challenges in maritime security (piracy, drug trafficking, IUU fishing).
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of Sri Lanka for India's maritime security and regional stability in the Indian Ocean.
Discuss the various facets of India-Sri Lanka cooperation in maritime security, including joint exercises and intelligence sharing.
Examine the challenges in bilateral relations, particularly concerning issues like illegal fishing and external influences.
Evaluate the effectiveness of India's 'Neighbourhood First' policy in building stronger ties with its maritime neighbors.
GS2.imageDescription: Naval ships of India and Sri Lanka conducting a joint exercise in the Indian Ocean.
GS2.trivia: The Palk Strait, a narrow strip of water separating India and Sri Lanka, is a traditional fishing ground, often leading to disputes.

GS2.headline: Parliamentary Session Sees Robust Debate on Key Legislations; Focus on Productivity
GS2.explainer:
• The ongoing parliamentary session witnessed robust debates on several key legislations, with both ruling and opposition parties engaging in discussions on national importance.
• Productivity of the Houses remained a central theme, with efforts to maximize legislative business and minimize disruptions.
• Bills related to economic reforms, social welfare, and environmental protection were introduced and deliberated upon, highlighting the legislative agenda.
• While some contentious issues led to temporary stalemates, overall efforts were made to ensure constructive parliamentary proceedings.
• The session underscored the importance of parliamentary democracy in India, serving as a platform for accountability and policy formulation.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Parliament and State Legislatures—structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these.
prelimsPoints: Functions of Parliament (legislative, oversight, deliberative).
Types of parliamentary sessions (Budget, Monsoon, Winter).
Mechanisms for legislative debate (bills, motions, resolutions).
Importance of parliamentary productivity.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of parliamentary debates in a democratic system and the challenges to ensuring productive legislative sessions in India.
Analyze the role of both ruling and opposition parties in shaping legislative outcomes and holding the executive accountable.
Examine the impact of disruptions and procedural issues on parliamentary functioning and suggest measures to enhance efficiency.
Evaluate the importance of robust legislative processes in strengthening India's democratic institutions and policy-making.
GS2.imageDescription: The interior of the Indian Parliament building during a session, with members debating.
GS2.trivia: The Indian Parliament consists of the President, the Lok Sabha (House of the People), and the Rajya Sabha (Council of States).

GS3.headline: Union Cabinet Approves New National Logistics Policy to Boost Economic Competitiveness
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet today approved the much-anticipated National Logistics Policy (NLP), aimed at reducing logistics costs and improving India's global trade competitiveness.
• The policy seeks to integrate various modes of transport, streamline regulatory frameworks, and leverage technology for efficient goods movement across the country.
